title: The labour market
summary:
## The big idea Why does a surgeon earn twenty times what a cleaner earns? Why can one dominant hospital chain pay nurses less than they're worth? The labour market answers with the same demand-and-supply machinery as any market — except here the 'price' is the **wage**, the 'quantity' is **employment**, and the buyers and sellers swap roles: **firms demand labour, households supply it**. ## The demand for labour: MRP Labour demand is a **derived demand** — no firm wants workers for their own sake, only for the output they produce and the revenue that output earns. So the value of a worker to a firm is their **marginal revenue product**: > **MRP = marginal physical product (MPP) × marginal revenue (price of the product, in competition)** If an extra barista makes 40 coffees a day that sell for \$3 each, her MRP is \$120 a day. A profit-maximising firm hires while workers add more than they cost — up to where **MRP = wage**. Because of diminishing returns, each extra worker's MPP eventually falls, so **MRP falls** — giving a **downward-sloping demand curve for labour**. Anything raising MRP (better training, new equipment, a higher product price) shifts labour demand right. **Elasticity of labour demand** is higher when: labour is a **large share of total costs**, capital can **easily substitute** for workers, the product's own demand is elastic, and the time period is long. ## The supply of labour Supply to an **occupation** depends on the wage *and* everything else about the job: qualifications and training needed (barriers that keep supply low — and wages high), job satisfaction, danger, hours, status. For an **individual**, a higher wage usually means more hours offered (the **substitution effect**: leisure just got more expensive) — but at high wages the **income effect** (rich enough to afford more leisure) can win, bending the individual's supply curve **backward**. **Why wages differ** — the wage-differential toolkit: differences in **MRP** (skill, productivity), **human capital** (education/training restricting supply), labour **immobility** (geographic and occupational), **compensating differentials** (extra pay for night shifts, danger, unpleasantness), union power, and **discrimination**. ## Competitive wage determination In a competitive labour market the wage settles where **market demand for labour (ΣMRP) meets market supply**; each individual firm is then a **wage taker**, hiring at that wage until MRP = W. ## Monopsony — when the buyer has the power A **monopsony** is a market with one dominant **buyer** of labour — a mining town's single mine, or the government hiring nurses and teachers. The monopsonist faces the whole upward-sloping labour supply curve, and that changes everything: to hire one more worker it must raise the wage — *for everyone already employed*. So the **marginal cost of labour (MCL) lies above the supply curve (ACL)**.
**What each part means:** the monopsonist hires where **MCL = MRP** (orange dot), giving employment **Lm** — but pays only the wage needed to attract that many workers, read *down* on the **supply curve**: **Wm** (yellow dot). Compare the competitive outcome (grey dot): wage **Wc** and employment **Lc**, both **higher**. The vertical gap at Lm between MRP (135-level) and the wage Wm is worker 'exploitation' — workers are paid less than the revenue they generate.
## Unions and minimum wages — and the twist - In a **competitive** labour market, a **trade union** (or a **minimum wage**) that pushes the wage above equilibrium creates **excess labour supply** — some workers gain, others can't find work. - In a **monopsony**, the same intervention can raise **both wages *and* employment** — a wage floor stops the employer holding wages below MRP, flattening its MCL up to the floor, so hiring expands toward the competitive level. This reversal is the single best evaluation point in the topic: *the effect of a minimum wage depends on the structure of the labour market* — and empirical studies (e.g. on the UK's minimum wage) often find little or no job loss, consistent with employer wage-setting power. - Unions bargaining with a monopsonist (**bilateral monopoly**) settle somewhere between the two power positions; unions can also *raise* MRP via training and cooperation. ## Why it matters The labour market carries market-structure logic into questions people actually argue about — pay gaps, union laws, the minimum wage — and it's a Paper 4 favourite: MRP calculations, the monopsony diagram, and 'discuss whether a minimum wage reduces employment'.
## ⚡ Quick revision - Labour demand = **derived demand**; hire until **MRP = wage**; MRP = MPP × MR; falls due to diminishing returns. - Labour-demand elasticity ↑ when labour share of costs is big, substitution easy, product demand elastic, long run. - Supply: wage + non-monetary factors + qualifications; individual curve can **bend backward** (income > substitution effect). - **Wage differentials**: MRP/skill, human capital, immobility, compensating differentials, unions, discrimination. - **Monopsony**: MCL above S; hires at MCL = MRP → **lower wage & employment**; workers paid < MRP. - Union/minimum wage: competitive market → possible unemployment; **monopsony → can raise wages *and* jobs**. - Bilateral monopoly: outcome depends on bargaining power.

keyTerms.definition: Demand for a factor that arises from demand for the goods it helps produce.
The extra revenue from employing one more worker: marginal product × marginal revenue.
A difference in wages between jobs or workers, due to skill, MRP, mobility or non-monetary factors.
Extra pay to offset unattractive job characteristics (e.g. danger or unsocial hours).
When higher wages eventually reduce hours worked as the income effect outweighs the substitution effect.
A market with a single or dominant buyer of labour, which lowers wages and employment.
An organisation of workers that bargains collectively to raise wages and improve conditions.
A legal wage floor to protect low-paid workers.

diagrams: **Competitive labour market:** downward MRP (labour demand) and upward labour supply; equilibrium wage and employment at their intersection.
**Monopsony:** supply of labour (= average cost of labour) with the marginal cost of labour above it; employment set where MCL = MRP, wage read off the supply curve → lower wage and employment.
**Trade union / minimum wage:** show a wage set above equilibrium and the resulting excess supply (unemployment) in a competitive market, or the employment-raising effect in a monopsony.
**Backward-bending supply curve:** individual labour supply rising then bending back at high wages.
tips: Define labour demand as **derived demand** driven by **MRP**, and state the hiring rule **MRP = wage**.
Draw and explain the **monopsony diagram** (MCL above supply → lower wage and employment) — a favourite exam topic.
Show the **union/minimum-wage effect differs** between a competitive market (possible unemployment) and a monopsony (can raise employment) — strong evaluation.
Use **wage differentials** (MRP, skills, mobility, compensating differentials, discrimination) to explain pay gaps.
Introduce the **backward-bending supply curve** via income vs substitution effects for A2-level depth.

channels.why: Clear labour-market, MRP and monopsony coverage.

keyConcepts.title: Trade unions and the minimum wage

revisionNotes:
# The labour market — quick revision

**Demand side**
- **Derived demand**: workers wanted for the revenue their output earns.
- **MRP = MPP × MR (product price)**; hire until **MRP = W**; diminishing returns → downward demand.
- Shift demand: productivity, technology, product price. Elasticity: labour cost share, substitutability, product PED, time.

**Supply side**
- To an occupation: wage + training barriers + job characteristics.
- Individual: substitution vs income effect → possible **backward-bending** curve.
- **Differentials**: MRP/skill, human capital, immobility (geographic/occupational), **compensating differentials**, unions, discrimination.

**Competitive market** — W and L where market MRP = market supply; firms are wage takers.

**Monopsony (dominant employer)**
- Faces whole supply curve → **MCL > ACL(=S)**.
- Hires at **MCL = MRP**, pays wage off **S** → lower W *and* L than competitive; wage < MRP ('exploitation').

**Unions & minimum wage — the structure twist**
- Competitive market: wage floor above equilibrium → excess supply (possible unemployment).
- **Monopsony: a wage floor can raise BOTH wage and employment** (flattens MCL) — key evaluation; UK minimum-wage studies found little job loss.
- Bilateral monopoly: bargaining decides.

**Exam habits** — calculate MRP carefully; draw monopsony with all three curves labelled; always condition minimum-wage answers on market structure.
questions:
# Practice questions — The labour market **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** The demand for labour is described as a 'derived demand' because it: - A — is derived from the supply of labour - B — depends on the demand for the goods labour produces - C — falls as the wage rises - D — is set by trade unions
▸ Show answer

B. Firms want workers only for the output (and revenue) they generate — demand for labour is derived from demand for the product.

**2.** An extra worker raises a firm's weekly output by 50 units, sold in a competitive market at \$4 each. The worker's MRP is: - A — \$50 - B — \$200 - C — \$4 - D — \$800
▸ Show answer

B. MRP = MPP × price = 50 × \$4 = \$200 per week. The firm should hire if the weekly wage is below \$200.

**3.** Compared with a competitive labour market, a monopsonist employer will pay: - A — a higher wage and employ more workers - B — a lower wage and employ fewer workers - C — a higher wage but employ fewer workers - D — the same wage but employ fewer workers
▸ Show answer

B. Because MCL lies above supply, the monopsonist stops hiring earlier (MCL = MRP) and pays only the supply-curve wage at that employment — both wage and jobs below the competitive outcome.

**4.** At very high wage rates an individual's labour supply curve may bend backward because: - A — the substitution effect of a wage rise outweighs the income effect - B — the income effect of a wage rise outweighs the substitution effect - C — marginal revenue product begins to fall - D — taxes rise with income
▸ Show answer

B. Higher wages make leisure more expensive (substitution → work more) but also make the worker richer (income → afford more leisure). When the income effect dominates, hours fall as wages rise.

## Paper 4 style — data response > In one country the government employs 80% of all nurses. Nursing wages have grown far more slowly than nurses' measured productivity, vacancies are chronic, and a third of trained nurses have left for jobs abroad or outside nursing. A nursing union demands a national minimum nursing wage 15% above current pay. The health ministry replies that higher wages 'must mean fewer nurses employed'. **(a)** Define 'monopsony'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain **two** pieces of evidence that the nursing labour market is monopsonistic. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse, with the aid of a diagram, the ministry's claim that a higher wage must reduce employment. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the union's proposed wage floor would benefit the country's health system. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A labour market with a single or dominant buyer of labour. [1+1]

(b) One employer hires 80% (dominant buyer); wages below productivity growth (paid < MRP — the monopsony signature); chronic vacancies + exit suggest wages held below market-clearing. Any two. [4]

(c) The claim assumes a competitive market (floor above equilibrium → excess supply). In monopsony, a wage floor flattens MCL up to the floor → the employer hires more: both W and L can rise toward the competitive point. Diagram with MCL, S, MRP, both outcomes. [6]

(d) For: raises retention and recruitment (stems emigration), moves pay toward MRP, fills vacancies → better care. Against: budget cost → possible cuts elsewhere; if set above the competitive wage it could overshoot; regional variation. Judgement: with clear monopsony evidence, a moderate floor likely helps; the 15% figure needs testing against MRP. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why wages differ between occupations. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the view that wage differentials simply reflect differences in workers' productivity. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) MRP differences, human capital/training barriers, immobility, compensating differentials, union power, discrimination — with examples (surgeon vs cleaner).

(b) For: competitive theory ties wages to MRP; returns to skill/education fit the data. Against: MRP is hard to measure (team output); immobility and barriers sustain rents; monopsony pays below MRP; discrimination and union power break the link; public-sector pay is administratively set. Judgement: productivity anchors, imperfections deviate.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with a diagram, how a trade union may affect wages and employment in a competitive labour market. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether a national minimum wage inevitably causes unemployment. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Union wage above equilibrium → employment falls along demand, excess supply appears; size of loss depends on elasticity of labour demand; unions may offset by raising MRP (training, productivity deals).

(b) Competitive model: yes if set above equilibrium — more so where demand is elastic. But: monopsony → can raise employment; small bites (floor below/near equilibrium) → little effect; efficiency-wage effects (motivation, turnover); empirical evidence (UK) finds modest job losses. Judgement: 'inevitably' is false — depends on level and market structure.
